coredata.dc:title: Phase change material thermal response under simultaneous charging and discharging process in an annular finned storage system with orientations: An experimental study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Khobragade, Sandip
Devanuri, Jaya Krishna
coredata.dc:description: Because of the sporadic supply of solar radiation and the need for uninterrupted energy, latent heat storage systems based on phase change materials (PCMs) can be a prominent method. A storage system that can simultaneously store and discharge thermal energy can meet the requirement. Therefore, a simultaneous charging and discharging (SCD) based latent heat storage system is considered for the study. Due to the low thermal conductivity of PCM circular fins have been considered for heat transfer augmentation. The effect of latent heat storage system (LHSS) inclination on temperature distribution, melt profile, average temperature, energy stored, exergy, and effectiveness are examined. The study revealed that the natural convection significantly influenced the thermal transport and differed corresponding to the inclination. The melting time is noted to be higher for the vertical unit and decreased with the decrease of inclination. The maximum energy storage (250 kJ), exergy efficiency (12.8%), and effectiveness (0.61) are obtained for horizontal configuration. The energy storage and exergy efficiency obtained for the horizontal position are 24.7 % and 32.36 % higher than the vertical configuration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change material
Inclination
Circular fins
Exergy
Effectiveness
Simultaneous charging and discharging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dimensions and thermocouple position of shell and tube heat exchanger.
Temperature distribution (axial direction) towards hot side with inclination.
Temperature distribution (axial direction) towards cold side with inclination.
Melt profile evolution over time with orientation.
Melt fraction vs time at different inclinations.
Average temperature of PCM with inclination.
Energy input by hot HTF and energy output by cold HTF.
Energy stored by the PCM with orientation.
Exergy efficiency of LHSS with inclination.

head.ce:title: Phase change material thermal response under simultaneous charging and discharging process in an annular finned storage system with orientations: An experimental study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Warangal, Warangal, 506004, Telangana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Warangal, Warangal, 506004, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Because of the sporadic supply of solar radiation and the need for uninterrupted energy, latent heat storage systems based on phase change materials (PCMs) can be a prominent method. A storage system that can simultaneously store and discharge thermal energy can meet the requirement. Therefore, a simultaneous charging and discharging (SCD) based latent heat storage system is considered for the study. Due to the low thermal conductivity of PCM circular fins have been considered for heat transfer augmentation. The effect of latent heat storage system (LHSS) inclination on temperature distribution, melt profile, average temperature, energy stored, exergy, and effectiveness are examined. The study revealed that the natural convection significantly influenced the thermal transport and differed corresponding to the inclination. The melting time is noted to be higher for the vertical unit and decreased with the decrease of inclination. The maximum energy storage (250 kJ), exergy efficiency (12.8%), and effectiveness (0.61) are obtained for horizontal configuration. The energy storage and exergy efficiency obtained for the horizontal position are 24.7 % and 32.36 % higher than the vertical configuration.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thermal energy storage particularly latent heat storage systems (LHSS) has notably drawn tremendous interest in balancing the energy demand and supply of cleaner energy. Over the decades, energy storage via phase change materials (PCMs) has been extensively researched on their thermophysical properties. PCM based LHSS is highly efficient and reliable because of its high energy storage capacity over the small change in temperature. The PCM store 5-14 times more energy with the same volume as compared to the sensible storage materials [1]. Due to this PCMs can be used in various applications such as heating and cooling of buildings [2, 3], thermal management of electronic systems [4, 5], textile [6], waste heat recovery [7, 8], etc.

However, the poor conducting nature of the PCMs resulted in limiting the energy storage or retrieval from the system [9], which reduces the thermal performance of LHSS during the charging and discharging process. Therefore, various heat transfer enhancement techniques are implemented to improve the performance of LHSS [10]. The most relevant approaches are the insertion of metal foam [11, 12], attachment of fins [10, 13], PCM encapsulation [14, 15], and dispersion of nanoparticles [16–18]. Among these, the employment of fins is an easy and efficient approach to enhance thermal performance [13]. In literature, various types of fins are taken into account viz. circular, longitudinal, tree-shaped, and pin fins. Ma et al. [19] carried out experimental analyses on a PCM heat exchanger with and without annular fins. It was observed that the annular fin heat exchanger showed better performance. Researchers employed circular fins and highlighted the significant enhancement of thermal transport. Cheng et al. [20] employed the circular fin in latent heat storage system where paraffin melting behavior was observed. They stated that when fin tube is replaced with un-finned tube the heat transfer rate is enhanced. Seeniraj et al. [21] suggested the fin-shell specifications and recommended the fin diameter should be equal to the shell diameter. This can be provided for better structure stability and also endorse cascade PCM arrangement.

Shahsavar et al. [22] studied the influence of fin parameters (thickness, location, and diameter) during the melting and solidification of PCMs. It was mentioned that the non-uniform distribution of fins gave a better performance during solidification. Furthermore, the melting time decreased by decreasing the thickness of the fin and the diameter of the fin up to a certain value. Ismail and Lino [23] analyzed the impact of radial fin diameter on discharging process. It was observed that 80 mm optimum fin diameter leads to a decrease in the solidification time. Ogoh and Groulx [24] highlighted the effect of fin number and reported that 15 fins are the optimized fin number. With a further increase in fin number, the maximum storage capacity of PCM was decreased. In line with this, Jmal and Baccar [25] also reported that the excessive number of fins affects the flow of PCM due to the smaller space available between fins. Yang et al. [26] also optimized fin parameters for optimal reduction of the PCM melting time. The optimized parameters observed were: the fin thickness/ length ratio = 0.0248, number of fins = 31, and the fin pitch/ length ratio = 0.0313. On a similar line, the same observation was reported by Pu et al. [27] and Santos et al [28]. Kalapala and Krishna [29] optimized the fin number to minimize the melting time and entropy generation. They highlighted the optimized values for fin diameter (80 mm) and fin number (15).

Based on the above analyses, the addition of fins is an effective method to improve the performance of LHSS. Fins, on the other hand, impact the flow of melted PCM which affects natural convection. The orientation of the LHSS also influences heat transport. Some researchers examined the inclination effect on the energy storage of LHSS without fins [30–36]. However, studies on the influence of orientation on heat transport for a finned LHSS are limited. Zhang et al. [37] measured the melting behavior of PCM with various types of fins namely annular, longitudinal, quadruple helical, and double helical fins in vertical and horizontal orientations of LHSS. The horizontal system revealed higher energy transport when quadruple helical and longitudinal fins were employed. Whereas, the vertical system was more efficient with annular and double helical fins. Mahdi et al. [38] studied the effect of inclination on LHSS with longitudinal fins. It was noticed that the inclination of the LHSS has a minor effect on the charging process. Recently, Kalapala and Krishna [39] investigated the energy and exergy analysis of LHSS with annular fins. They considered vertical (0°), 60°, 30°, and horizontal (90°) orientations for their study. It was observed that exergy and energy efficiencies were higher for vertical orientation.

Studies on the LHSS mainly focused either on the melting or solidification process with the idea that the system will be melted or solidified uninterruptedly. However, in a practical application, the requirement can exist during the non-availability of the energy source. Therefore, the simultaneous charging and discharging (SCD) process is important to eliminate the switching of charging/discharging during continuous operation. In this regard, Murray and Groulx [40] reported the first study in 2014, where the SCD process is carried out in annular LHSS. They compared the temperature distribution for different HTFs flow rates. It was noted that the HTFs flow rate has an insignificant influence on heat transport. Omojaro and Breitkopf [41, 42] studied the propagation of a solid-liquid interface during the SCD process for a horizontally positioned concentric tube heat exchanger. The heat input is given by the electric heater and at the same time, discharged by the airflow over the shell. Joybari et al. [43] numerically investigated the thermal behavior of PCM with and without fins. Based on the initial condition (internal or external heating) the solid-liquid interface and temperature distribution were observed. Also, Joyabri et al. [44] developed the melt fraction tracking correlation by comparing pure conduction and natural convection under SCD. Khobragade and Devanuri [45, 46] investigated the energy and exergy efficiencies without fins under the SCD process. They noted that the position of hot and cold HTF tubes significantly affects the thermal performance of LHSS. Recently, Fang et al. [47] carried out an experimental study on the horizontal configuration of shell and tube heat exchangers under the SCD process.

From the above review, it is observed that less study is available on the LHSS which is based on the simultaneous charging and discharging process. Previous works pertaining to the simultaneous charging and discharging process were performed on the triplex or concentric tube heat exchangers. In this study, an SCD based LHSS is designed with annular fins to enhance thermal performance. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this is the first work to evaluate the performance of circular finned LHSS with different inclination angles under the SCD process. In the present research article melt profile, temperature distribution, average temperature, energy storage, exergy analysis, and heat transfer between the cold and hot fluids are analyzed at different orientations.

The pictorial view of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 . The experimental setup consists of a shell and tube heat exchanger, centrifugal pump, hot bath, cold bath, digital flow meter, flow adjust valve, angle adjuster, and PID controller. Commercially available lauric acid is filled in the shell side and water is used as HTFs, which is made to flow through the tube by using a centrifugal pump. The shell has 101.6 mm (diameter) and 254 mm (length) with 5 mm thickness made up of polycarbonate. Two tubes are used to pass the hot and cold HTFs from hot and cold baths respectively. PID controllers are used to maintain the temperature of hot and cold baths at the desired value. The inner diameter and thickness of the HTF tubes are 12.7 mm and 1.4 mm. Four circular fins of diameter 88.9 mm and 0.8 mm thickness are implemented. Stainless steel is used for fins and tube material. To change the orientation an angle adjuster is provided. In this study, 13 K-type thermocouples are installed. Thermocouples are arranged such that both axial and radial direction temperature measurement is possible. The position of thermocouples and dimension details of LHSS are shown in Fig. 2 . The thermophysical properties of lauric acid and the instruments’ uncertainty are given in Table 1 .

Initially, the PCM is filled in the complete space of the heat exchanger. Before starting the experiment it is ensured that the experimental setup is at the atmospheric condition and there is no leakage. During the experiment, the hot bath is maintained at a prescribed temperature using a PID controller. At the same time, the cold bath is also maintained at the desired temperature. Then, the hot and cold HTFs are circulated through tubes simultaneously. A digital flow meter is installed to monitor the volume flow rate of HTFs. The temperature at various points is recorded by DAQ (Data acquisition system). To analyze the thermal performance with orientation four different inclinations viz. 90°, 60°, 30°, and 0° are taken. 90° and 0° correspond to vertical and horizontal positions respectively.

An experimental setup has various elements and each one introduces some error in the performance and calculation. These errors are used to calculate the experimental uncertainty associated with a system. In this study uncertainty analysis suggested by Robert Moffat [48] is adopted. The uncertainty of the components considered is given in Table 2 and the uncertainty for energy transport is calculated to be ±12.6%. The uncertainty of the energy transport is given by Eq. 1
(1)
δ
E
i n
E
i n
=
(
δ
m ˙
m ˙
)
2
+
(
δ T
T
)
2
Where
′
m ˙
′
is the mass flow rate of the HTF and ‘T’ is the temperature difference between the inlet and outlet of the HTFs.

Experiments have been performed on an LHSS with a simultaneous supply of hot HTF (charging) and cold HTF (discharging) maintained at temperatures of 80 °C and 27 °C. The flow rate of the HTFs is perpetuated at 1.4 LPM. Four different inclinations viz. 90°, 60°, 30°, and 0° are considered for the study. 90° and 0° correspond to the vertical and horizontal orientations of the system. Results are presented in terms of temperature distribution, the average temperature of PCM, energy stored, heat input and output by HTFs, exergy, and effectiveness of LHSS.

During the initial duration, the tube absorbs energy from hot HTF, and then the energy transfer to solid PCM takes place. This results in a rapid rise in temperature near the hot tube. This behavior will be the same regardless of the orientation which is illustrated in Fig. 3 . The figure highlights the temperature rise of PCM at three different locations i.e. 1, 4, and 7. The location of the thermocouples 1, 4, and 7 along the hot tube is shown in Fig. 2. The time required to reach the solidus temperature is shown in Fig. 3. In the vertical position, the time needed for thermocouple 1 to reach the solidus temperature is 1970 s. It can be noted that the duration to attain the solidus temperature increases with the decrease in orientation. This is due to the positioning of the hot tube which goes below the cold HTF with the decrease in inclination. As the melting process start, due to buoyancy, the liquid PCM starts to move up. Hence, in Fig. 3 it is observed that thermocouple 7 which is at the bottom portion of LHSS requires more time to reach the solidus temperature as compared to the upper half (1 and 4). The bottom portion thermocouple i.e. 7 shows the time required to attain the solidus temperature is minimum for 90° orientation followed by 60° and 30°. For the horizontal system, all thermocouples 1, 4, and 7 are exactly below the hot tube and all the curves coincide. Also, it can be observed that the thermal gradient between the lower and upper portions decreased with the decrease in orientation. The thermal transport in the lower half of the LHSS can be inferred to be conduction due to the least increase in temperature.

Similarly, Fig. 4 shows the temperature distribution in the axial direction towards the cold side. The location of these thermocouples (3, 6, and 9) along the cold tube is shown in Fig. 2. As time progress, recirculation for liquid PCM is created in upper portions. The liquid PCM tries to move towards the upper section due to natural convection. This is evident in Fig. 4 where the time taken to reach the solidus temperature is less for thermocouple 3 in the vertical system. The delay in attaining solidus temperature for cold side thermocouples indicates the heat transport between melted PCM and cold HTF. With the decrease of inclination as the position of thermocouples 3, 6, and 9 move towards the upper section, the melted PCM moves up due to buoyancy. Therefore, by comparing the rise of temperature for thermocouple 6, the time taken to reach the solidus temperature is minimum for horizontal followed by 30°, 60°, and 90°.

By comparing Figs. 3 and 4 it can be observed that in the vertical position (90°) the temperature near thermocouple 1 has reached the solidus temperature earlier than thermocouple 3. As thermocouple 1 is near the hot HTF tube the temperature rise is earlier than thermocouple 3. But as the inclination angle decrease i.e. from 90° to 0° the time taken to reach solidus temperature near thermocouple 3 becomes lesser than thermocouple 1. With the decrease in inclination, the hot HTF tube moves towards the lower portion and the cold HTF tube to the upper sections of the heat exchanger. The movement of the melted PCM to the upper sections and less temperature heavier PCM to the lower portions due to buoyance causes this behavior. A similar kind of effect was noted on comparing the temperatures near thermocouples 4 – 6 and 7 – 9. It can be noted that as the inclination angle decreases, the temperature near the hot tube did not reach the solidus temperature due to the settlement of the melted PCM towards the cold tube side which can be observed from Fig. 5 .

From thermal behavior, it is noted that the inclination of LHSS significantly influences the temperature distribution during the SCD process due to corresponding changes in natural convection. Therefore, natural convection must be taken into consideration. The temperature distribution in the axial direction indicates that for the vertical system (90°), the strength of natural convection is gradually increased and unsymmetrical distribution of temperature is noted. Whereas for horizontal (0°) the strength of natural convection is higher in the upper section due to which the upper portion attains solidus temperature in less time. The optimal inclination of the heat exchanger cannot be established based on the temperature distribution alone. To obtain a better understanding, other factors such as average temperature, and energy storage should be considered which are discussed in the next section.

The melt fraction in each control volume is calculated depending on the temperature in that control volume. There are three possibilities for the melt fraction value which are given as
(2)
c i
=
{
0
T <
T s
T −
T s
T l
−
T s
T s
≤ T ≤
T l
1
T >
T l

The melt fraction is calculated by using the following equation
(3)
c = ∑
w i
.
c i
.
wi is the weighting factor and calculated by the control volume of the thermocouple (Vi)/ Total volume (Vt) for each thermocouple.

Fig. 5 shows the propagation of the solid-liquid interface at 7200 s, 14400 s, and 21600 s of the melting process at all inclinations. At the initial stage, PCM near the hot HTF tube melted first and due to the buoyancy effect, liquid PCM tends to move upwards. But because of the presence of fins, the liquid PCM is made to flow along the fins' surface. Further, the liquid PCM starts to move upwards through the gap between the fins and shell. Thus, the melted portion is observed near the fin's surface. For the vertical position, the melt profile is noted to form a sawtooth profile. With inclination, the melted PCM is obtained near the fins and solid PCM remained between the two successive fins. As time progress (14400 s), more amount of melted PCM is observed in the horizontal position when compared to the vertical, 60°, and 30° orientations. This is due to the intensification of buoyancy which escalated the natural convection and melting process. At 21600 s, more solid PCM can be observed in a vertical (90°) followed by 60°, 30°, and horizontal (0°) position. This behavior can be inferred to be more convection strength caused due to the positioning of the cold HTF tube above the hot HTF tube. The placing of the cold HTF above the hot HTF tube strengthens the natural convection and results in effective thermal transport from the hot HTF tube to the cold HTF tube. Positioning of cold HTF tube towards the upper portion ensures an additional advantage when compared to the vertical position due to the more exposure to melted PCM. It can also be seen that a thin layer of unmelted PCM is present around the cold HTF tube. This results in lowering the thermal transport from melted PCM to the cold HTF. From Fig. 6 it can be noted that during the initial stage there is no variation in melt fraction. But with the progress of time, the influence of buoyancy can be noted with inclination due to which variation in melt fraction can be observed.

Fig. 7 shows the average temperature of PCM within LHSS with inclination. The average temperature is calculated based on the method suggested by Kalapala and Devanuri [32]. The control volumes are created around each thermocouple. As the control volume of each thermocouple (Ti ) is not the same a weighting factor (wi) (Control volume of the thermocouple (Vi)/ Total volume (Vt)) is calculated for each thermocouple. Based on the weighing factor, the average temperature is calculated as
(4)
T
a v g
= ∑
w i
.
T i
.

From Fig. 7 (a) it is noted that there is no variation of average temperature with inclination at the preliminary stage due to the conduction. After the commencement of the melting process, a noticeable difference is observed in average temperature. This is because of natural convection which differs correspondingly with the inclination. Also, it is noted that the average temperature increases rapidly between 1000 s and 4000 s for the 30° and horizontal configurations due to the intensification of the natural convection which drives a higher amount of melted PCM toward the upper half of the LHSS. However, the maximum average temperature is found in the vertical (90°) configuration followed by 60°, 30° and horizontal (0°). From Fig. 5, the horizontal position has more melted PCM as compared to the other inclinations and should result in a higher average temperature for the horizontal configuration. But from Fig. 7 the maximum average temperature is observed in the vertical position. The reason for attaining lower temperature for horizontal in comparison to vertical is due to the positioning of cold HTF above the hot HTF. The melted PCM moves up due to buoyancy and makes good contact with the cold HTF tube which leads to effective thermal transport from the PCM. From Fig. 7 (b) it can be observed that horizontal (0°) and 30° inclinations took the same time to attain the steady-state condition. Here, the steady-state condition can be inferred to no further melting of PCM. After attaining steady-state condition the PCM no longer retains heat from the hot tube. As a result, direct thermal transport between hot and cold heat transfer fluids will occur.

The input and output energies are calculated by Eqs. (5) and (6).
(5)
Q input
=
m ˙
h
C
p , HTF
(
T
in , h
−
T
out , h
)
(6)
Q output
=
m ˙
c
C
p , HTF
(
T
out , c
−
T
in , c
)

Here,
m ˙
h
and
m ˙
c
(L/min) are the flow rates of hot and cold HTFs. Cp,HTF (kJ/ kg K) is the specific heat of water. Tin,h, Tout,h, Tin,c and Tout,c (K) are inlet temperature of hot HTF, outlet temperature of hot HTF, inlet temperature of cold HTF and outlet temperature of cold HTF respectively.

Fig. 8 presents the energy input and output over time with different inclinations. The variation in energy input and output is caused due to the temperature gradient between the inlet and outlet temperatures of HTFs. From Fig. 8 it can be observed that the energy input decreases for the initial duration then increases and later becomes constant. This can be referred to as the variation of thermal transport between the PCM and hot HTF. In the early stage, the conduction mode of heat transfer is dominated, and hence the energy input decreases. As natural convection comes into play more heat is absorbed by the PCM and energy input increases. Energy output is initially zero due to no temperature difference between the cold HTF and PCM. As the temperature difference increases, the energy output continues to increase from zero and attain a steady condition over a period of time. As the melting process continues the thermal transport from melted PCM to cold HTF increases. This resulted in more temperature gradient for the cold HTF. As a result, more energy is carried by the cold HTF. However, the energy output is restricted due to the thermal resistance offered by solid PCM around the cold HTF tube as shown in Fig. 5. With the decrease of inclination from 90° to 0° the energy input and output can be noted to increase. The increase in the strength of natural convection with the decrease of inclination can be referred to as the increase in energy input and output.

The energy stored by the PCM at a given orientation is calculated based on Eq. (7) [32].
(7)

Where m (kg) is the mass of PCM. Ti, Tini, Ts and Tl (K) are the instantaneous temperature of PCM, initial temperature of PCM, solidus temperature, and liquidus temperature respectively. ci is melt fraction of PCM. Cps and Cpl (kJ/ kg K) are the specific heat of solid and liquid PCM.

Fig. 9 shows the variation of energy stored by the PCM at different inclinations. The result shows that the trend of energy storage for all inclinations is similar. It is observed that the highest value of energy stored is 250 kJ for the horizontal (0°) position and followed by the 30°, 60°, and vertical (90°) systems. It can be noted that the energy storage by the PCM depends on the amount of melted PCM. From Fig. 5 it can be observed that the unmelted PCM is less in the case of horizontal configuration. Therefore, the maximum energy stored is observed in the horizontal position. From Fig. 9 it can be noted that with the progress of time energy storage has come to a steady-state condition. As discussed in section 3.3 the steady-state condition refers to the direct heat transfer between the hot and cold HTFs which indicates that energy storage by the PCM is not prevalent.

The effectiveness of the heat exchanger is defined by Eq. (8) and is presented in Fig. 10 .
(8)
Effectiveness
( ∈ )
=
Energy
output
by
the
cold
HTF
Maximum
energy
input
=
(
T
out , c
−
T
in , c
)
(
T
in , h
−
T
out , h
)

Effectiveness is the ratio of energy output from the cold HTF to the energy input from the hot HTF. It is observed that effectiveness increases over time. The maximum effectiveness is noted for the horizontal position. This is because of the maximum temperature difference between inlet and outlet temperatures of hot and cold HTFs observed for the horizontal system. As the inclination increases from horizontal (0°) to vertical (90°) the temperature difference decreases and the effecveness of the system decreases. The maximum effectiveness of LHSS at the end of 21600 s is 0.61 at 0° (horizontal) orientation.

In the performance evaluation of LHSS, exergy analysis is one of the important parameters to be considered along with the energy analysis. Therefore, it is required to estimate the exergy efficiency. The rate of exergy input given by cold and hot HTFs and exergy stored/released can be calculated by Eqs. (9) – (13).

Here Ta and Tavg is the ambient temperature and average temperature of PCM respectively.

Similarly, exergy efficiency under SCD can be defined by Eq. (13).
(13)
Exergy
efficiency
( η )

Δ

Δ
E

in
(
hot
HTF
)

Fig. 11 provides the variation of exergy efficiency at different inclinations under the SCD process. The exergy efficiency increases with the decrease in orientation from vertical to horizontal. The maximum exergy efficiency for the vertical (90°) position is 9.67 % and is observed to increase with the decrease of inclination. For 60°, 30°, and 0° the maximum exergy efficiencies are 10.7, 12.4, and 12.8 % respectively. It can be noted that the exergy efficiency depends on the average temperature of PCM and the temperature difference between the inlet and outlet temperatures of HTFs. Owing to the maximum average temperature which is higher in the vertical position the exergy efficiency is less. This can be due to the non-uniform temperature along the cold HTF tube for the vertical position. The hot melted PCM moves up due to buoyancy whereas the low temperature PCM settles down due to gravity. This gradient along the length of the HTF tube decrease with the decrease of inclination from 90° to 0°

To understand the advantage of using fins the present study is compared to earlier work [45] where the thermal performance is analyzed without fins. It is observed that the usage of circular fins showed a significant increase in thermal performance. The energy storage and exergy efficiency are found to be greater by 30.43% and 57.7% in the vertical position and in the horizontal position by 3.03% and 71.81% than the LHSS without fins. Moreover, for the present size of LHSS and fins, the horizontal system is the most preferred for the SCD process.

The heat transport behavior in the thermal storage system under the SCD process is examined experimentally by employing fins. The influence of orientation on thermal transport is ascertained by considering various parameters such as melt profile, average temperature, energy input and output, energy stored, effectiveness and exergy efficiency. From the thermodynamic analyses the following conclusions are drawn:
•
The maximum average temperature is observed for the vertical position. With the decrease of inclination i.e. from 90° to 0° the melted PCM moved up due to buoyancy and dissipated the heat to cold HTF. This phenomenon resulted in a lower average temperature for horizontal configuration (0°)
•
The strength of natural convection is observed to get intensified gradually for vertical configuration (90°). While for the horizontal position (0°) the natural convection got intensified quickly in the upper portion of the LHSS.
•
The energy input to the PCM is significantly influenced by the orientation of the LHSS.
•
Due to the formation of a solid PCM layer on the cold HTF tube, the energy transport to the cold HTF is less in comparison to the hot HTF. The solid PCM layer on the cold HTF tube increases the thermal resistance causing deterioration in energy transport.
•
It is observed that the highest value of energy stored is 250 kJ for the horizontal (0°) position and followed by the 30°, 60°, and vertical (90°) systems.
•
The maximum effectiveness and exergy efficiency at the end of 21600 s are determined to be 0.61 and 12.8 % for horizontal configuration.
•
The energy storage and exergy efficiency are found to be greater by 30.43% and 57.7% in the vertical position and in the horizontal position by 3.03% and 71.81% than the LHSS without fins.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
